title: Ageratina subferruginea
taxonomy: Ageratina subferruginea is classified within the family Lamiaceae, commonly known as the mint family. This classification places Ageratina subferruginea alongside other plants such as oregano and thyme. The genus Ageratina is part of the tribe Nepeteae, which consists of flowering plants found in the Americas. Ageratina subferruginea is also classified within the order Lamiales, a large and diverse group of flowering plants. Further classification within the family Lamiaceae places Ageratina subferruginea as a member of the order Lamiales.
uses: Ageratina subferruginea, also known as the 'Ferruginous Snakeroot', has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits. The plant is valued for its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which are believed to aid in wound healing and reducing pain. In some cultures, Ageratina subferruginea is used to treat respiratory issues such as bronchitis and coughs, due to its expectorant properties. Additionally, the plant is said to have antimicrobial properties, which may help in fighting off infections. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy of Ageratina subferruginea for medicinal uses.

It is said to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help in reducing pain and inflammation. preparations: Ageratina subferruginea, also known as False Ironweed, is sometimes used in herbal preparations to make teas, tinctures, and infusions. These preparations are often used to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal problems. intro: Ageratina subferruginea, also known as Mexican mint or fernleaf ageratina, is a perennial plant native to Mexico, characterized by its fern-like leaves and small, white flowers. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its various health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of fever, cough, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Ageratina subferruginea include sesquiterpenes, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat various health conditions, including colds, flu, and bronchitis.
